questions.question: What is the key advantage of P-frames over I-frames?
options: P-frames encode only the difference from a reference frame, so they are much smaller
P-frames do not need any reference
questions.explanation: P-frames use motion-compensated prediction to encode only the residual difference from a past reference frame, drastically reducing bitrate.

questions.question: Why does the decode order differ from the display order in MPEG?
options: Because the encoder sorts frames by size
Because B-frames need both a past and future reference, so references must be decoded first
questions.explanation: B-frames depend on both past and future reference frames. The encoder must buffer and send reference frames before the B-frames that reference them, so decode order diverges from display order.

questions.question: What does a motion vector represent?
options: A (dx, dy) offset pointing to the best matching block in the reference frame
questions.explanation: A motion vector is a displacement that tells the decoder where to find the matching block in the reference frame, avoiding the need to encode the full block.

questions.question: What happens when a P-frame is lost during transmission?
options: All subsequent frames that depend on it (directly or indirectly) are corrupted
questions.explanation: P-frames and B-frames that reference the lost P-frame cannot decode correctly. This error propagates until the next I-frame resets the prediction chain.
